query: can CT scan predict the level of CSF block in tuberculous hydrocephalus

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Treatment / Management -- Shunt Surgery in Tuberculous Meningitis
retrieved_docs.content: Vellore and modified Vellore grading systems have been validated for patients with TBM with hydrocephalus to aid in surgical decision-making and predict the success of VPS surgery and ETV. The grades vary from 1 to 4, increasing in disease severity. The modified version incorporates the Glasgow Coma Scale score. The management of patients with grade 4 TBM is still contentious. [93]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Treatment / Management -- Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: IRIS is believed to result from a delayed type-IV hypersensitivity reaction triggered by the significant release of mycobacterial antigens and increased tumor necrosis factor-α and CSF lymphocytes or protein levels. IRIS manifests with CN deficits due to nerve entanglement with inflammatory exudates. Conditions like communicating hydrocephalus, spinal arachnoiditis, and syringomyelia may also develop. However, the clinician's judgment is crucial in excluding other infections or potential treatment failure.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- History and Physical -- Tuberculous Meningitis and HIV Infection
retrieved_docs.content: HIV coinfection does not affect CNS-TB's clinical manifestations, CSF picture, or prognosis. However, HIV-seropositive TBM may have a normal CSF, with radiographic clues holding greater importance in such cases. Cryptococcal meningitis is a close differential diagnosis and may sometimes occur concurrently. Intracerebral tuberculomas are more likely to occur in HIV patients than in the general population, often as multiloculated tubercular abscesses. CNS lymphoma or toxoplasma encephalitis closely mimics the condition. However, cisternal enhancement, basal ganglia infarction, and communicating hydrocephalus favor a CNS-TB diagnosis. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroimaging is vital in diagnosing suspected CNS-TB cases, helping ensure a timely and correct diagnosis. Neuroimaging ideally must include the entire neuroaxis. The evaluation modalities include brain computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance imaging, with contrast-enhanced MRI having a superior delineating ability. Newer techniques further enhance MRI's diagnostic reliability in atypical or difficult cases (see Image . Tuberculous Meningitis on Magnetic Resonance Imaging). [43] The diagnostic triad of TBM consists of the following: Extensive basilar leptomeningeal enhancement and exudates, found in 38% to 89% Hydrocephalus, detected in 60% to 75% Cerebral infarcts, seen in 15% to 28%, followed by tuberculoma in another 27% of cases [44] [45] [46]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: CNS-TB's prognosis is dismal even after therapy. TBM's mortality ranges between 9.8% and 57%, as it is associated with distressing levels of neurologic sequelae that disproportionately afflict children. [109] Various mortality and neurological morbidity predictors have been identified, including age, symptom duration, Glasgow Coma Scale score, absence of headache, CSF protein level, arachnoiditis extent, presence of complications, and Medical Research Council staging at presentation. [110]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Clinical Diagnosis of Tuberculous Meningitis
retrieved_docs.content: Identifying active extraneural infection occasionally aids in determining a presumptive diagnosis. Chest radiographs are beneficial in most pediatric TBM cases and approximately 50% of adult cases (see Image . Pulmonary Tuberculosis). Laboratory analysis may indicate mild anemia, lymphocytosis, and hyponatremia. A positive tuberculin test offers supportive evidence, notably in children, though a negative result does not rule out the diagnosis. Funduscopy may also uncover papilledema and choroid tubercles, aiding diagnosis (see Image . Tuberculoma on Funduscopy). [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Tuberculosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging studies : Up to 60% of cases with extrapulmonary TB may not have evidence of lung TB and may have a normal chest x-ray. [74] [75] [50] Chest x-ray or high-resolution computed tomography (CT) of the chest demonstrates findings of pulmonary TB, including hilar lymph node enlargement, pulmonary infiltrates, and cavitation. [76] High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) chest has a better sensitivity (96%) of picking up active pulmonary tuberculosis than chest x-rays (48%). [77] [78] Another study noted that "the sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive values of thorax HRCT in determining the activity of the illness (pulmonary tuberculosis) were found as 97%, 86.7%, 94.2%, and 92.9%, respectively." [77] Magnetic resonance imaging is preferred for TB of central nervous system and tuberculous spondylitis. [78] Positron emission tomography-computed tomography (PET-CT) may help in evaluating the response of pulmonary and extrapulmonary TB to antitubercular therapy. [79] Neuroimaging should be done in patients with choroidal tuberculoma to rule out brain involvement. [80]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Clinical Diagnosis of Tuberculous Meningitis
retrieved_docs.content: Newer molecular techniques help confirm the presence of MTB in CSF, which is often challenging. Still, a definitive diagnosis is not always achievable, necessitating presumptive and prompt treatment initiation to mitigate delay-related risks. Investigating epidemiological factors and personal risk variables is crucial, even though such data may be limited in adults. Notably, active tuberculosis exposure can be identified in 70% to 90% of children with TBM.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: TBM exudates have certain areas of predilection. Optochiasmatic arachnoiditis is almost pathognomonic of TBM. [47] The other areas include the interpeduncular fossa, perimesencephalic region, cisterna ambiens, and suprasellar and Sylvian cisterns. Exudates are best discerned on MRI fluid-attenuated inversion recovery sequences, while enhanced meninges are more visible on postcontrast spin echo MRI (see Image. Tuberculous Meningitis on MRI).

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Cerebrospinal Fluid Examination and Culture
retrieved_docs.content: The CSF cell number may be 500 to 1,500 cells/mm3 in 20% of cases. A transient polymorphonuclear predominance or mixed pleocytosis may also be observed in the first week of illness. However, predominant lymphomononuclear pleocytosis is the typical CSF finding in TBM. CSF protein concentration varies from 100 to 500 mg/dl. About 25% of cases may have less than 100 mg/dl of protein, and 10% may have greater than 500 mg/dl. Values exceeding 1 g/dl is characteristic of severe adhesive spinal arachnoiditis and portends a poor prognosis. The CSF glucose level is less than 45 mg/dl in 80% of cases. However, unlike acute pyogenic meningitis, CSF glucose is never undetectable.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Cerebrospinal Fluid Examination and Culture
retrieved_docs.content: A pellicle or cobweb may form during gross CSF examination, which is representative but not pathognomonic of TBM. A xanthochromic CSF indicates unusually high protein content. CSF cytological analysis typically shows the total cell count between 100 and 500/mm3 in the majority. Less than 100 cells/mm3 are present in 15% of cases. Pleocytosis may be absent in people of advanced age or with miliary tuberculosis-related CNS-TB or HIV coinfection.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculous Meningitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroimaging can further aid in the diagnosis of TBM. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has demonstrated superiority to computed tomography (CT) scans, as it is of higher quality for assessing the brainstem and spine in detecting TBM. [15] Imaging can assess cerebral infarcts, cerebral edema, and meningeal enhancement. CT imaging is best used to rule out the emergent complication of TBM-related hydrocephalus that can result in the need for immediate neurosurgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: On CT, intense basal cisternal enhancement may appear as a characteristic spider leg appearance (see Image . Tuberculous Meningitis on Computed Tomography). [48] Imaging features are specific (95–100%) for TBM only when interpreted in combination. However, these findings have reduced sensitivity if taken individually.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningitis -- Evaluation -- Computed Tomography (CT) of the Head before Lumbar Puncture
retrieved_docs.content: Blood work should include blood culture, serum electrolytes as the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) is not uncommon, serum glucose, renal and liver function, and testing for HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Advanced radiological imaging techniques are usually of great assistance in making presumptive diagnoses. Vasculitic infarcts, cranial nerve (CN) palsies, multiple neurological deficits, and hydrocephalus frequently complicate CNS-TB. A strong clinical suspicion is typically enough to start prompt antitubercular therapy. The 4-drug regimen of isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol with adjunctive corticosteroid reduces morbidity and mortality. However, CNS-TB diagnosis and management may be complicated by drug resistance, immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS), and HIV coinfection.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculous Meningitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: TBM is considered the deadliest form of MTB infection. [17] TBM carries a mortality rate between 20% and 67% with anti-tuberculosis treatment and is fatal without treatment. [3] Patients at the extremes of age and patients with HIV co-infection carry the highest mortality. [17] [18] The prognosis of TBM depends on the patient's neurologic status at the time of initial presentation and the timeliness of the initiation of anti-tuberculous agents. [2] Patients who develop hydrocephalus secondary to MTB also have a poor prognosis, even with neurosurgical intervention. [9] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site” includes the specific procedure referred to in Arabic as “التحويلة البطينية البريتونية” (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). This type of shunt diverts cerebrospinal fluid from the brain’s ventricles to an extracranial location, typically the peritoneal cavity, and is employed in the management of conditions such as hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is a neuro‑anatomical disorder in which excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway, directly involving the Brain, Nerves and the broader nervous system (nervový systém). The condition is clinically associated with dementia (خرف) and senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي) and often requires neurosurgical intervention (جراحة الأعصاب) such as insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt. Specific shunting procedures include ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية), ventricular‑atrial shunt (تحويلة بطينية وعائية), and ventricular shunt to nasopharynx, all of which illustrate the close relationship between shunting techniques and CSF circulation anatomy. Neuroimaging also includes مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي (abnormal brain echo) for assessing hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions. Therapeutically, management of استسقاء الدماغ involves procedures categorized as “Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt,” a نوع of تحويلة بطينية وعائية (ventricular‑vascular shunt) performed by جراحة الأعصاب (neurosurgery). This procedural group is the parent of “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which encompasses the specific التحويلة البطينية البريتونية, and is related to the synonymous “Ventricular shunt to nasopharynx,” a direct procedure site within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 234
retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, these relationships integrate neurological disorders (hydrocephalus, dementia, seizures, dizziness), their neurosurgical interventions (ventricular shunts), underlying etiologies (malignant brain tumor, abnormal CSF), pharmacologic treatment (meclizine), and a comprehensive imaging framework anchored by routine chest radiography that supports diagnostic evaluation across the brain, chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is a neurological condition that affects the head (hlava) and is clinically associated with both general dementia (خرف) and senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي). Computed tomography of the head, performed as a computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head, is a diagnostic procedure that directly targets the head (hlava). This head CT scan is also clinically associated with dementia and co‑occurs with senile dementia, highlighting its relevance in the evaluation of cognitive decline. Moreover, the computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head serves as a primary mapping reference for more extensive imaging studies, including computerized tomography of the brain and chest, computerized tomography of the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and computerized tomography of the brain, chest and abdomen. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of head imaging in identifying and managing hydrocephalus‑related cognitive disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 234
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging pathways are organized around the routine chest X‑ray, which serves as a reference standard for a wide range of computed tomography (CT) examinations. From the chest X‑ray, CT protocols are mapped to chest CT, abdominal CT (التصوير المقطعي للبطن), combined chest‑abdomen, chest‑abdomen‑pelvis, brain‑chest, and brain‑chest‑abdomen studies, with or without intravenous contrast. The brain component of these combined studies follows the standard head CT protocol (computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head) and belongs to central nervous system imaging procedures (إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي). Encounters generated because of a routine chest X‑ray are explicitly related to that imaging study, underscoring its role as the methodological basis for subsequent advanced CT imaging. Collectively, these relationships integrate neurological disorders (hydrocephalus, dementia, seizures, dizziness), their neurosurgical interventions (ventricular shunts), underlying etiologies

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is classified under the broader category “Brain and Nerves” and is listed as a member of the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term). The condition involves the head (hlava), reflecting its anatomical location within the brain. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with both general dementia (خرف) and senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي), indicating that patients with hydrocephalus often present cognitive decline characteristic of these dementias. Computerized axial tomography of the head (computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head) is a diagnostic imaging procedure derived from the more extensive “Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium.” The CT head is performed directly on the head (hlava) to visualize intracranial structures. This imaging modality is also clinically associated with dementia (خرف) and co‑occurs with senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي), underscoring its utility in evaluating patients who have cognitive impairment potentially related to hydrocephalus or other neurodegenerative processes. Thus, hydrocephalus, its placement within neurological taxonomy, its relationship to dementia, and the role of head CT imaging are interlinked components that together inform diagnosis and management of patients presenting with cognitive decline and intracranial pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1084
retrieved_docs.content: Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) includes استسقاء الدماغ (hydrocephalus) as one of its members. استسقاء الدماغ is a condition that involves the anatomical region hlava (head) and is clinically associated with both خرف (dementia) and خرف شيخوخي (senile dementia). Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium is the primary source from which computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head is derived. The computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head directly images the site hlava, belongs to the broader category إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي (central nervous system imaging procedures), and is clinically associated with خرف as well as co‑occurring with خرف شيخوخي. Within إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي, an additional child procedure is مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي (abnormal brain echo map). Together, these relationships highlight how hydrocephalus, head‑focused CT imaging, and related CNS imaging modalities are interconnected with cognitive disorders such as dementia and senile dementia, underscoring their relevance for diagnosis and patient management.

Overall, the network provides a structured, multilingual representation of radiologic and neurosurgical procedures—ranging from basic X‑ray to advanced CT protocols, computer‑assisted surgery, and ventricular shunting—organized by modality, anatomical focus, and clinical indication, and linked to relevant disease states to support comprehensive patient evaluation and standardized documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1019
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is classified as a neurological disorder within the broader category of Brain and Nerves and is listed as a member of the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term). It is considered a condition of the head (hlava) and therefore falls under the anatomical domain of the head. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with both general dementia (خرف) and senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي), indicating that patients with ventricular enlargement are at increased risk for cognitive decline and age‑related dementia. Computerized axial tomography (CT) of the head (computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head) is a primary imaging modality that maps to more extensive studies such as “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium.” The CT head scan is directly performed on the head (hlava) and is categorized under central nervous system imaging procedures (إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي). It is clinically associated with dementia and co‑occurs with senile dementia, reflecting its utility in detecting structural brain changes that underlie cognitive impairment. Within the hierarchy of CNS imaging, the CT head procedure has a child procedure identified as an abnormal brain echo map (مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي), further emphasizing its role in detailed neuro‑imaging assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are both primarily mapped from the concept of a computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. In clinical contexts, a computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head frequently co‑occurs with خرف شيخوخي (senile dementia), highlighting the relevance of head CT imaging in the assessment of elderly patients with cognitive decline and informing diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

Clinically, استسقاء الدماغ frequently co‑occurs with التحويلة البطينية البريتونية (ventriculoperitoneal shunt) and is strongly linked to خرف (dementia) and خرف شيخوخي (senile dementia). retrieved_docs.content: Insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt is classified as a ventriculoatrial shunt procedure, reflecting its function of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to the atrium for the management of hydrocephalus and related conditions.

Additional neurosurgical considerations include إحصار عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular blockade) and the spectrum of related neuropathologies such as diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور). Overall, the interconnected knowledge emphasizes that استسقاء الدماغ is a central nervous system disorder with defined anatomical locations, characteristic clinical associations (especially with dementia), specific neuroimaging modalities (CT head and abnormal brain echo), and a set of neurosurgical interventions essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and comprehensive patient care.

The CT head scan, classified under إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي (central nervous system imaging procedures), directly targets hlava and is clinically associated with خرف and co‑occurs with خرف شيخوخي, reinforcing the relationship between neuroimaging findings and dementia in hydrocephalus patients. Within the same imaging category, abnormal brain echo mapping (مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي) is listed as a related child procedure, highlighting that sonographic assessment of atypical brain patterns is also part of comprehensive CNS imaging. Together, these anatomical, procedural, and imaging relationships provide an integrated framework for the diagnosis, neurosurgical treatment, and clinical implications of استسقاء الدماغ within neurological and neurosurgical care.
